Q1.label: Question 1
Q1.scenario: Company B is a health and care insurance company that has written Private Medical Insurance (PMI) business for many years in Country Y. Company B calculates its statutory reserves on a Solvency Il basis. The marketing director of Company B has proposed introducing a new annual health check option for its PMI business. The health check is a 30-minute in person appointment with a healthcare professional and involves a range of health tests and questions. Policyholders who take the annual health check option will also be given a wearable electronic device that will record data about the policyholder such as daily step counts, heart rate, calories burned and weight. The policyholder will be able to upload the data from their electronic device to their smartphone or computer to monitor their health. The information collected by the policyholder's wearable device will be shared with Company B. Policyholders who take up the health check option and use their wearable electronic device will receive a discount on their PMI premiums. The policyholder's premium would not be directly affected by the health data provided from the health checks. There will be no charge to the policyholder for the annual health check, but there will be a small upfront charge to cover the cost of the wearable device. The marketing director believes that the introduction of annual health checks and wearable electronic devices will lead to better health outcomes for the policyholder as it will encourage policyholders to lead a healthier lifestyle. The marketing director believes that Company B will also benefit from healthier policyholders.

(i).question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the marketing director's proposal for:

Advantages for Company B: Potentially improve the profitability of Company B's PMI business;
This could be due to increased new business sales if customers like the health checks/wearable technology.
It could also be due to improved claim experience.
Offering the health checks etc could be a marketing advantage for Company B
particularly if Company B has first mover advantage in the market.
Potential reputational advantage in the market if Company B is seen as an innovative company.
Information gathered though the health checks can help the insurer to understand the overall level of health for the PMI portfolio better and can be used to help set more accurate assumptions.
Better understanding of the portfolio will potentially allow more accurate pricing for the portfolio as whole.
If policyholders like the new health check this could lead to increased policyholder loyalty.
Company B could consider removing or reducing initial underwriting requirements as it now has the annual health check, which could reduce its costs.
Increased sales due to the marketing effect of the wearable technology may allow for reduced expense loading per policy, thereby reducing premiums
Disadvantages for Company B: It may be expensive operationally for Company B to administrate the health check process.
The cost of administrating the health check process could exceed any benefits Company B may have from improved experience....
which would have an adverse impact on Company B's profits.
There may be a number of operational challenges in collecting the data from the wearable technology
such as devices not working properly
or policyholders may not have the correct partnering technology e.g. a smart phone to pair with the device.
Introducing the wearable technology is likely to require significant investment by Company B, the cost of which may not be recouped.
The cost of collecting, storing and monitoring the data from the policyholder's electronic devices could also be significant.
If sales are significantly higher than expected this could lead to capital strain on the insurer.
The benefits in improved policyholder health may not emerge.
Poor public relations risk may increase e.g.  backlash on ethical use of data, GPS location tracking.
Data risk may increase e.g. there may be a data breach or increased cyber-attack risk.
Operational risk increases e.g. ability to source the wearable tech at agreed/suitable prices.
Counterparty risk may increase e.g. the supplier of wearable tech goes bankrupt; the provider of the App fails to provide a good service.
Capital requirements may increase - due to the above increased risk exposures.

Advantages for Policyholders: The health check/wearable tech could encourage the policyholder to lead a healthier lifestyle, which will be good for the policyholder's long-term health.
The policyholder is getting the health check for free.
The combination of the health check/wearable tech could be seen as a major value added by the policyholder.
The policyholder will be happy they are receiving a reduction in their premiums to reflect their healthier lifestyle.
Disadvantages for Policyholders: It may be logistically difficult for some policyholders to get the physical the health checks.
Not all policyholders will want to pay for the wearable technology.
Not all policyholders will want to have regular health checks.
Some policyholders may feel annual health checks and having to regularly use wearable technology is intrusive.
Some policyholders may have concerns about the large amounts of data the insurer is collecting on them
and concerns about how secure their sensitive personal data is.
Some policyholder may not have smartphones or computers and feel they are not being treated fairly.
Insurance access is tied to 'selling' personal data.
The policyholder may not have the right to 'not share' the data following the check-up.
The policyholder may not have the right to dispute the findings of the check-up or wearable technology.
The policyholder may have penalties if for example, loose the wearable technology or fail to attend a health check-up appointment.

(ii).question: The chief actuary of Company B has asked about the potential impact of introducing the annual health check on Company B's statutory reserving assumptions for its PMI business. Discuss the impact of introducing the annual health check on the valuation assumptions for Company B's Solvency Il Best Estimate Liabilities for its PMI business.

Overall: The projections in the BEL calculations should allow for all expected decrements and policyholder actions, including lapses.
Company B must take into account all relevant available data, both internal and external, when arriving at assumptions that best reflect the characteristics of the underlying insurance portfolio.
So if Company B expects the annual health check to have a material impact on the experience of its PMI business then this impact will need to be reflected in Company B's BEL assumptions.
It is likely that some judgement will be required when assessing the impact of adding the health check on PMI experience.
The overall impact on future experience will depend on range of factors such as:
The expected take up of the annual health check
Whether policyholders are honest about whether they are meeting the health targets
How challenging the health targets are
All BEL assumptions should be best estimate, with no prudential margins, so no margins for prudence should be added to the amended assumptions.
Health check option take up: A best estimate assumption will need to be made about the proportion of policyholders that will take up the health check.
Morbidity Rates: The health checks may encourage policyholders to make lifestyle changes, and encourage more health living
which could improve policyholder morbidity rates.
Mortality Rates: Healthier living may also improve policyholder mortality rates
Although policies are short term so unlikely to have much effect
Business mix: The health checks may change Company A's business mix such that its target market is healthier.
It is possible that some groups of policyholders may not have access to smartphones which could impact the business mix
for example, older people may not have access to smartphones, so it is possible that the proportion of younger people will increase.
Claims Costs: The annual health check could increase the potential for early intervention for policyholders in the event the policyholder has a health condition
Potentially reducing the severity of the health condition.
This could potentially reduce future PMI claim costs.
However, claim costs may be brought forward (due to being aware of conditions earlier)
which could increase overall claim costs.
There is a risk that claim costs may increase significantly as the health check may identify potential illnesses or conditions that require further tests which could increase claim costs.
The health check may also make policyholders aware of less serious issues that they may have, and as a result they may claim for these when they might not otherwise.
Lapse Rates: Increased policyholder loyalty due to the introduction of the health check could lead to an improvement in (i.e. lower) lapse rates.
Expenses: If the upfront cost of the introducing the device exceeds the upfront charge to the policyholder upfront policy costs could increase.
Ongoing policy expenses are likely to increase to reflect the ongoing cost of maintaining the device.
Ongoing Monitoring: Company B will need to monitor its experience in future years to check whether the expected benefits from the annual health checks actually materialise.

Monitoring the information gathered though the health checks will help Company B to get a better understanding of the overall level of health improvements of the PMI portfolio from the annual health check and can be used to help set more accurate assumptions in the future leading to provide stability in premiums going forward which is preferred by customers and distributors.

(iii).question: Company B has been approached by an external third-party company that has offered to carry out the annual health assessments and collect and monitor the data from the wearable devices. The third-party company has carried out similar services in another country and is currently setting up a number of medical centres in Country Y. The annual health assessment will be carried out in person at one of the third-party company's medical centres in Country Y. Outline the factors Company B should consider in deciding whether to use the third-party company.

Details about the outsourcing company: Company B will want to understand details about who the third-party company is.
Company B will need to obtain full references and credentials before such parties are employed.
Company will want to understand who the senior management of the third-party company are.
Confirmation the third-party company has regulatory permission to carry out the responsibilities it has been asked to do.
Operational: Company B will want to ensure that the third party is competent to carry out the health assessment and guidance.
It will consider factors such as:
How similar are the activities the third party has done in other countries to what Company B wants done
How qualified the medical professionals who will carry out the health assessments are
Company B will also want to consider how the assessment process will work in practice
And to understand how the third party will deal directly with actual or potential clients in the Company's name.
How will Company B and the third party interact
Will each companies' IT security protocols be able to interact appropriately.
How many health centres will the third party have.
How well does the location of the health centres match with Company B's target market.
What service levels does the third-party work to.
Systems and Controls: Given the type of information being collected Company B will want to understand what systems and controls are used
and the risk management processes the third-party company has in place
Who has access to the data.
Clarity over who the third party can share personal information about the policyholder with and any restrictions on how that data will be shared.
Regulatory: Understand the regulatory requirements Company B has to meet to outsource these processes to a third party.
Reputation: Can Company B get references from other parties using the third party for similar services.
How successful was the third party in implementing other similar arrangements.
Financial: Understand the charging mechanism of the third party.
Is the charge per case, a flat charge, a volume related charge etc.
What will the term of the deal with the third-party company be.
What happens at the end of the term.
Does Company B have the option to renew the term automatically?
Monitoring: Consideration will need to be given about how Company B will monitor how the third party is meeting Company B's requirements.

(iv).question: Company B agreed to implement the health check option and outsourced the administration of the health check option to the third-party company. Three years after the health check option was added the management of Company B carried out an analysis of the experience of PMI business since the health check option was added. The management of Company B are pleased with the take up of the option, and the level of new business sales. However new business profitability for its PMI business has been significantly lower than expectations. The finance director of Company B has proposed that Company B should introduce an annual charge for the health check for both existing and new business. Discuss the finance director's proposal.

Profitability: Company B is currently incurring additional costs for managing the annual health check on its PMI business but not receiving any additional income for the health check
introducing an annual charge could go some way towards meeting the cost of the health check
Consideration needs to be given to whether the charge for the health check will fully cover the cost of the health check or partially cover the cost.
Introducing the annual charge could put off prospective policyholders from taking out a new policy
which could adversely impact new business sales and overall product profitability.
Company B will need to consider the impact on the take up rate for the health check option.
Existing Policyholder response: Some existing policyholders may value the annual health check and be willing to pay for it
particularly if the annual charge is less than the policyholder would have to pay if they tried to get an annual health check themselves.
However, some existing policyholders may be unwilling to pay the additional charge
even if policyholders are willing to meet some of the costs of the additional health check they may only be willing to partially meet the costs of the health check.
New policyholders over the last three years will have bought the PMI product on the basis that there will be no additional charge
therefore these policyholders may feel that Company B is being unfair by introducing the charge for the health check.
Introducing the charge for existing policyholders could cause Company B reputational damage
so Company B may feel that for this reason it is not possible to introduce an additional charge for these policyholders.
Consideration will need to be given to the discount that existing policyholders will be receiving.
Will the policyholder still receive the discount but this will be offset by the premium increase?
Lapse Rates: If existing policyholders are unhappy with the introduction of the annual charge for the health check this could lead to an increase in existing policyholders lapse rates.
New Policyholders: A new charge could be introduced for future new business
however, this could adversely impact new business sales
and have an adverse impact on product profitability.
Consideration would also need to be given to the competitive position of Company B
other companies may also offer an annual check. Consideration will need to be given to how much they charge (if anything) for the health check.
Operational: There are a number of additional operational costs of introducing the charge for the health check.
There will be the costs of implementing this change.
The new charge will need to be explained in policy literature
and also possibly disclosed in any pre-sale literature.
Competitors: If Company B introduces the new charge how will it impact the competitiveness of Company B's PMI terms.
If competitors are charging for health checks too then that would potentially make it easier for Company B to implement the change.
If competitors don't charge for health checks is there a risk of anti-selection from policyholders who could lapse their Company B policies
and take out a similar policy with a competitor.
Other options: There could be other options available other than introducing the charge.
For example, carry out health checks less frequently.

Q2.label: Question 2
Q2.scenario: Country A has a small private Long Term Care (LTC) insurance market that has been slow to develop over the past 15 years. Country A has an aging population and a publicly funded health system with no private provision of healthcare services. Access to government funded long term care is means-tested. The majority, 75% of the adult population in Country A, self-fund their long term care needs. Country B has private provision of both healthcare and long term care services, with no public provision of these services. Private LTC insurance was launched in Country B 15 years ago, and since then the market has grown and is now well established with 75% of the adult population purchasing some level of private LTC insurance cover.

(i).question: Describe the main product features of pre-funded LTC insurance.

Benefit: Accessing advice while in need of care
Alternatively, may provide cash in a lump sum or as an annuity certain subject to LTC need continuing
Cash benefit might be level or have fixed, index-linked or capped increases.
Increases could be provided in and out of claim.
Benefits are paid for life or a specified term.
Underwriting: At time of purchase and at claim
Bought by those not currently in need
Claim trigger: Based on failure to meet a certain number of ADLs or mental impairment
A deferred period, where ADL's fail to be met, may be in place before a claim is triggered.
Premium: Regular - May stop after a certain age
May be option for premium waivers
A death benefit may be included where a claim is not paid or is paid for only a short period of time.
Premium paid in advance (often many years) before any care needs.

(ii).question: Contrast the main product features of immediate needs LTC insurance to those shown in part (i) above.

Benefit: Given larger premium upfront the policyholder may be more interested in the benefits of:
The long-term indemnity nature of the benefit
The access to advice about care needs
Increasing benefits can only be applied in claim.
Underwriting: Bought by those already in need
Claim trigger: Benefits start immediately as already qualify.
No deferred period as already in poor health
Premium: Can be retrospectively e.g. equity release
Reviewable or guaranteed not applicable as single premium
A death benefit may be included where a claim is paid only for a short period of time.
Premium is paid at the time care is required.

(iii).question: Describe possible reasons why the LTC insurance market may be more developed in Country B than Country A.

General: Likely to be more insurers in Country B as the system is private, compared to A where it is all public. So there is more opportunity to sell LTC products in Country B.
With more players in the market in Country B this may create more competition and so more innovation in product design.
Insurance companies in Country A may have focused on selling other types of product than LTC which have been easier/ more profitable.
There may also be more skilled personnel like medical underwriters, actuaries who specialise in health care as the sector is private in Country B.
Demographic: Country A has an ageing population, so to date the number of citizens requiring LTC has been small.
Cultural environment: Country A may have a cultural norm of intergenerational living e.g. grandparents live with younger members of the family as they age and care needs develop.
Country B may have a very high perception of dignity around care needs e.g. they would like their needs to be met by professional services.
Country A citizens may feel as though they have already paid for their health and care services; even though they might not be entitled to care.
With no public provision, citizens of Country B may be very aware of their care needs and the complexity of provision and so seek insurance to minimise the burden
Whilst Citizens in Country A may be less aware.
Citizens in Country A may be averse to buying LTC for a range of reasons
For example, LTC products may have a poor reputation in Country A
Or insurance companies that sell LTC business in Country A may have a poor reputation.
Economic environment: Citizens of Country A may be more affluent than those in Country B and therefore may be better able to fund their long-term needs without purchasing LTC insurance.
Interest rate - may be higher in Country B making pre-funded premiums more competitive.
Inflation rate - Country B may have higher inflation.
Medical inflation - might be more controlled in country B as there may be competition to keep prices down and agreed contracts with providers.
Investment returns in Country B may be higher, making premiums more affordable.
Income tax may be lower in B, making it easier for people to afford insurance premiums.
Cost of living may be lower in Country B making it easier to afford insurance premiums.
Government incentives: Provision of health services being publicly funded may mean people are more able to save for their own care rather than pay for insurance.
Insurance premium tax may be waived in country B.
Corporation tax may be lower in B for insurers, freeing up capital for growth of LTC.
It might be legislated in Country B that some level of care is purchased.
The means testing could be perceived as generous by the population but as 75% are self-funding this suggests there is a need. So, citizens must be very able to self-fund.
There may be government incentives around this such as a tax-free savings vehicle for future care needs.
There might be tax relief for individuals who have LTC insurance in country B e.g. income or inheritance tax relief.
Regulatory and capital requirement envir: The regulator in Country A may have more capital-intensive solvency requirements than B, making the product overall less profitable.
There may have been poor reputation around LTC products in Country A following high profile complaints.

In Country A, regulatory restrictions on risk factors that can be used for pricing may make it unattractive to healthier lives, making the business mix less healthy overall - driving up morbidity risk and so premiums.
Country B's regulator may be very good at promoting the solvency and good customer service experienced making LTC providers trusted with large financial products.
Sales environment: There may be a preference for guaranteed premiums in Country A which may make the product expensive, whereas Country B may have reviewable premiums as a norm.
Distribution networks may be better at selling products in Country B, given the wide private provision in the health and care sector.
The level of private provision may create more insurance products and so also create more opportunities for cross selling LTC in Country B.
Country B may offer pre-funded LTC as part of the employee benefits.
LTC is a large financial decision; there may be a lack of trust in the government in Country A
Or other reason for a lack of stability in the sales environment which deters the trust required for the large financial decision to be made in Country A.

(iv).question: The government of Country A is concerned that its aging population will mean more of its citizens will have insufficient funds to meet the costs of long-term care and that an increasing proportion of the population will require financial support from the government to meet the cost of their long term care. The government of Country A is therefore considering encouraging insurance companies based outside of Country A to sell LTC insurance in Country A. Company XYZ is the market leading health and care insurance company for LTC insurance in Country B. The government of Country A has asked several health and care insurance companies based outside of Country A, including Company XYZ, to start selling LTC insurance in Country A. Company XYZ's main LTC product provides benefits on an indemnity basis in return for annual premiums payable until care is required. Company XYZ is considering if it will start writing pre-funded LTC insurance in Country A. Suggest possible reasons why Company XYZ should start selling pre-funded LTC business in Country A.

Sales/Profit: XYZ thinks it can write profitable business in Country A.
Levels of profit may be much higher in Country A than Country B
particularly if levels of competition are much lower in Country A than Country B.
Writing more business allows Company XYZ to spread its expenses and overheads over a larger number of policies.
Competition: Whilst the market is small currently it is possible that it could grow significantly
and Company XYZ could potentially get a significant share of the market.
Capital: Company XYZ may get a diversification benefit at a global level of writing business in Country A and Country B
Which could lead to reduced capital requirements.
It may be much more capital efficient for Company XYZ to write more profitable business in Country A than lower profitable business in Country B
Capital requirements may be lower in Country A than Country B.
Expertise: Company XYZ may gain more exposure to LTC business in an alternative market that could improve its overall expertise about LTC business.
Company XYZ could leverage its knowledge and experience of LTC business in Country B to gain an advantage over its competitors in Country A.
Admin: Company XYZ might have some spare capacity in the team in Country B to take on the business opportunity.
Risks: Company XYZ may be looking to capture larger/wider experience data for LTC to understand the risks of LTC business better (though noting the countries will have significant differences) to improve product design or sales in Country A.
Regulation: The government of Country A may offer some regulatory advantages if Company XYZ were to sell LTC business in Country A;
Other: If 75% of the adult population self-fund, it means there are assets held by the population which insurance industry may wish to help manage and generate income from.

(v).question: Discuss the factors Company XYZ should consider in determining if it is appropriate to price LTC business sold in Country A using the same pricing basis as is used for LTC business in Country B.

Claims Experience: Consideration will need to be given to any material differences between expected claims experience in Country A and Country B
and this will need to be allowed for in the pricing basis.
Company XYZ will want to understand any expected differences in experience by categories including age, gender, occupation, smoker status and region.
It will be important to consider any differences between the cost of claims in Country A and Country B…
…..as this will be important for assessing the claims reserves…
….and average claims will be important if the product is indemnity based.
Consideration should be given to whether there are any material differences in the cost of care between Country A and Country B ....
….and whether exposure is concentrated in different specific locations in Country A compared to Country B.
This may be because of the distribution of state and private facilities.
Consideration should be given to whether there are any differences between claims inflation rates between Country A and Country B.
Mortality and morbidity experience: Company XYZ will need to consider any differences in mortality and morbidity experience between Country A and Country B:
Mortality rates in Country A have been improving.
For pre-funded long-term care, this translates to both:
a larger proportion of lives reaching an age where they are likely to need care
and a likely reduction in impaired life mortality.
Company XYZ will need to consider the differences in future mortality improvements in Country A and Country B.
Lapses: Company XYZ will need to consider any differences in expected lapse experience, by factors such as:
Life Expectancy: The life expectancy in Country A might be lower and as such there may be less need for LTC in older years.
The life expectancy in Country A might be higher but these are also healthy years of life expectancy due to the publicly funded health care system which might be performing well.
Country B might have a shorter life expectancy for those requiring care and as such the expected timeline of the annuity payment is shorter.
Country B may have more medical advances which reduce the long-term probability of needing care.
For example, private provision driving innovation, compared to public provision of health care.
Expenses: XYZ will need to consider any differences in per policy expenses:
This will need to be divided by acquisition expenses, policy administration expenses pre-claim, claim assessment expenses, claims management expenses.
XYZ will need to consider any differences in expense inflation between claims from Country A and Country B.
Country B is a bigger market so there may be greater economies of scales, and therefore lower per policy expenses for business written in Country B.
Sales Costs: There may be differences in sales costs between Country A and Country B which will need to be reflected in the pricing assumptions.
Taxation: There may be differences in taxation which could impact assumptions for business written in Country A and B, for example
Differences in taxation on investment income
Differences in tax relief on insurance company expenses
New Business Volumes and Mix: XYZ will need to consider any differences in the profile of new business between business written in Country A and Country B
for example, differences in the volumes of new business.
Data: Country A has a limited LTC market so there is likely to be limited data available
whilst Country B has a more developed LTC market
and XYZ will a lot more experience of LTC business in Country B and therefore much more confidence about future experience than it will have for business in Country A
so XYZ may wish to have higher margins for LTC business in Country A.
XYZ may want to consider a range of data sources when considering the likely experience in Country A and any differences with its experience in Country A.
Other data sources for Country A could include
This data would need to be adjusted to ensure relevant to this insurer/product.
Investment Income: XYZ would need to consider any differences in investment income between assets in Country A and B.
Competitive Position: There may be lower levels of competition in Country A than Country B
which means there is scope for XYZ to have higher margins in Country A than would be acceptable in Country B.

Q3.label: Question 3
Q3.scenario: Company A is a large health and care insurance company in Country Z. Company A specialises in, and sells large volumes of, individual Critical Illness (CI) business.

Solution: The asset share is the retrospective accumulation of:
past premiums,
less expenses,
and the cost of cover,
at the actual rate of return of the assets
The accumulation could be done for single contract or a group of contracts
It is also referred to as the 'earned asset share' or 'retrospective earned asset share'

(ii).question: Company A has recently reviewed its existing business and found that longer duration policies (i.e. policies that have been in force for 10 years or more) written on guaranteed premium terms, have an asset share that is greater than expected. Discuss why the asset share may be greater than expected on longer duration policies.

Investment Performance: Investment performance may have been better than expected on longer duration policies.
This would have had a greater impact on longer duration policies than shorter duration policies.
Equities may have been used to back the CI liabilities and there may have been a 'bull' market since that business was written
which therefore allowed for good returns on the premium income.
A regulatory change may have enabled the insurer to take a more aggressive investment strategy than anticipated
so the insurer could have changed from using gilts and cash reserves only to equity and gilts
leading to higher investment returns than originally expected.
Tax rates in the asset share calculation could have been lower than anticipated.
For example leading to higher than anticipated investment growth.
Expenses: Insurer A's actual expenses could have been lower than originally anticipated.
This could be for a number of different possible reasons such as:
Expense inflation may have been lower than expected over the longer term.
Due to efficient management of Insurer A's operating expenses
These costs were lower than projected. For example:
Insurer A may have outsourced some operations leading to cost savings.
Insurer A may have adopted technology to reduce its costs - e.g. moving from paper based to electronic records.
Ongoing deductions from the asset share for advisor commission payments may have been lower than expected over the years.
Staff salary costs could have been lower than expected.
Staff numbers may have reduced over time to manage that portfolio.
Marketing costs could have been lower than expected.
Claims may have been lower than expected, and therefore the associated claims expenses are lower.
Upfront underwriting costs may have been lower than expected with fewer cases referred for ratings or review.
Overheads may have been spread over a larger number of policies than anticipated, meaning per policy costs were lower than expected.
Lapse rates may have been lower than expected in the early years for longer duration policies, meaning ongoing expenses were lower than expected.
Premiums: Ratings for non-standard policies may have included a significant margin.
Rated policies were a significant part of the sales.
More smoker business may have historically been written and these rated premiums included a comfortable margin.
It is less likely that rated lives will lapse or review their product and therefore, these more 'profitable' policies over time form a higher proportion of the longer duration policies.

Sales - it may be that historically the distribution channel was different e.g. undertaken by the 'travelling/door to door' salesperson which was unlikely to lapse or be reviewed. This sales approach may have resulted in less competition at the time of sales and so allowing for profitable premiums.
Business mix - cross subsidies in premiums may have been present. Those who remain may have been the loaded risk-segments in the cross subsidies.
Cost of cover: The morbidity rates charged by Insurer A may have been lower than originally anticipated
this could be because Insurer A over-estimated morbidity charges at outset and morbidity rates were reduced over the duration of the policy.
Reinsurance costs may have been reduced after years of good experience materialised, reducing the cost of cover.
Relatively more sales occurred in segments where the experience over longer durations was better than the morbidity risk estimated, than not.
Although Insurer A specialises in CI, other products sold may have provided good diversification across risks which allowed for a reduced capital requirements and so reduced cost of cover.

(iii).question: The economy in Country Z has been in recession for the last 5 years. This economic environment has placed downward pressure on premiums across all health and care insurance products. Reviewable premiums have replaced guaranteed premiums as the industry norm for new CI business sold in Country Z. Explain why insurance companies may have adopted reviewable premiums for new CI business sold in Country Z.

Stakeholders: Reinsurers may have changed the norm in the market by requiring reviewable premiums to gain reinsurance under a quota treaty
Reinsurers may have changed the norm by only offering reviewable reinsurance premiums, leaving the insurer holding the risk of the guarantee which may not be accepted
Management may have changed the risk appetite across all lines of business.
The insurer may be seeking a higher internal rate of return which can be more easily managed on an annual basis with reviewable terms.
Competitors: Competitors may have increased sales in the market on reviewable terms.
If the insurer was looking to transition to these terms, it is a good opportunity to adopt the market norm.
The insurer may be forced to adopt these terms, otherwise anti-selection on the guaranteed terms may be an increased risk
Economic: The economic uncertainty lends itself toward frequent risk monitoring and reviews in assumptions.
Assumptions reviews which can be followed up by premium alterations may reduce the 'unfunded' capital requirements.
Asset returns no longer support guaranteed premiums, due to the recession.
Uncertainty regarding future economic outlook may reduce the risk appetite of the insurer.
Expenses may be more volatile in an uncertain economic climate.
Long term contracts to manage expenses may not be possible due to economic uncertainty, making guarantees less appealing to the insurer.
Regulation: Capital intensity may be lower on reviewable terms.
Recent poor press on guaranteed premium terms by the regulator has reduced sales and increased complaints.
Insurers may be moving away from these terms to reduce regulatory review pressure on guarantees.
Liability: Often an economic recession can have an impact to increase morbidity rates in future years.
With poorer outlook or more uncertainty over morbidity trends, the insurer is looking to move away from guaranteed terms.
Anti-selection - if others in the market move, the insurer will need to adapt so as not to be selected against.

(iv).question: Recently, Company A has made the following changes: • new CI business will be written exclusively on a reviewable premium basis the investment strategy for the assets backing CI business has been updated. Justify why Company A has updated its investment strategy.

Solution: The investment strategy may require a change as it is no longer fit for purpose.
Or as part of the regular actuarial control cycle review process.
Certain internal risk control triggers may have been met
E.g. may have changed reinsurance treaty structure, triggering an investment strategy review.
An update may be needed to take on more opportunity for growth, given the high asset share to cover existing liabilities.
Specific update to investment strategy such that assets held are a better match for the nature of the premium income change
E.g. reviewable premiums may cause more lapses, changing the lapse probability and cashflows
E.g. reviewable premiums better match the changeable nature of outgo cashflows, reducing the level of need for 'matching' in the assets.
Specific update to better match economic outlook change:
E.g. Higher inflation with the recession
E.g. Higher interest rates to combat inflation
E.g. Lower sales with recession
E.g. Assets values more volatile in recession
E.g. Assets returns lower in recession
E.g. Assets default probability higher in recession

(v).question: The chief investment officer of Company A has asked for additional resources to carry out the work to implement the updated investment strategy. Outline the tasks Company A will need to undertake as part of implementing the updated investment strategy.

Solution: Notify and meet to discuss stakeholder involved:
Fund managers
Reinsurers
The Board
Shareholders or Policy holders if mutual
Regulator
Answer any questions that might arise from stakeholders.
Gain sign-off to proceed, as required.
Update the internal documentation.
Update the external/public documentation.
Hire staff with necessary skills to implement the investment strategy specifics.
Tender with consultancies with investment expertise to assist in the implementation of some specific elements.
Tender with reinsurers to assess if financial solutions are available/affordable to access.
Work with the reinsurer to adjust any existing treaties, if financial reinsurance is part of the investment strategy.
Discuss implications of any changes with different areas of the business:
Valuation - to update valuation assumptions as needed
Pricing - to update pricing assumptions as needed
Risk - let risk logs be updated as necessary
Accounts - update accounts to know new income, dividend, fees.
Work with existing fund managers to agree new fee structures as required.
Tender to fund managers to assess which can best meet the new investment needs.
Adapt the investment strategy to allow for any lack of asset availability.
Schedule disinvestments so as not to create significant 'shifts' in the market.
Schedule disinvestments so as not to take on too much risk in a one-day trade.
Schedule and execute investments - as above.
Ensure fungibility to allow for implementation.
Work with corporate banks to set up derivatives as needed.
Set up appropriate review cycles to ensure the strategy is working as expected.
